06 Step 4 Set your Target - YouTube.mp4
All right, so the next step is to actually set your targets. Now, targets are just another word for goals. So this is you and your team declaring in advance what 'winning' looks like. And it's vital that you do this because it's impossible to get where you're going if you don't know where you're supposed to be. Now, if you recall in the previous step, I had you back test your data for at least the previous month. If you did this, then it should be fairly easy for you to create a target based on your existing baselines. If your benchmarks are fairly close to your targets, then maybe you set your targets at 10 to 20% over your current benchmarks. If you're way behind, maybe you decide that you need to double your benchmarks or even more.
The point is you aren't just. you're looking back to look forward now. There are two things that you need to keep in mind when setting your targets: first, don't dictate to your team leaders what the target should be. It's critical at this stage that they truly own their metrics. So while you can push them a little bit, avoid the temptation to set unrealistic targets because this will not only demotivate your team, it'll actually cause them to lose trust in this very process. Second, understand that you will be wrong. I'm gonna say it again, okay? You ready for it? You will be. Wrong. On average, it takes companies at least one quarter— that is, 12 full weeks— before they're setting targets with really any degree of accuracy. So set this. Expectation with both yourself and your team. The goal at this stage is not perfection. It's not really even accuracy. The goal is learning. And you will learn more about your company than you know today by simply going through this process. So here's your mantra. Learning Now! Accuracy later. Once you have set your appropriately inaccurate targets, you're ready to move on to the next step.
